title: Death on the ice: The mystery and tragedy of the Franklin Expedition

passage.title: Death on the ice: The mystery and tragedy of the Franklin Expedition
passage.text:
1. Beginning in the 15th century, the dream of a “Northwest Passage”—a sea route across Canada that would [word] trading opportunities between Europe and Asia—lured hundreds of European explorers to the Americas. It would take more than 400 years for the dream of the Northwest Passage to finally be realized. Not only did the search for the route take centuries, it also claimed the lives of 129 men in one of the greatest mysteries in the history of Arctic exploration. 

###One last chance

2. John Barrow, Second Secretary of the [word] of the United Kingdom from 1804 to 1844 CE, sent numerous expeditions in search of the Northwest Passage. He was certain it existed, as he believed that sea ice could not freeze. So at age 82, Barrow decided to organize one last journey. With a hopeful heart, he set about making preparations for his final expedition.

###Not-so-promising beginnings

3. From the start, the [word] was plagued by challenges. Barrow’s first choice to lead the journey had grown weary of polar expeditions and refused the post. His second had promised his new wife that he would remain close to home. Others were rejected by the Admiralty for an array of reasons. Reluctantly, Barrow selected his sixth choice, Sir John Franklin, to lead the expedition.

4. If the captain of the expedition was less than Barrow had hoped, the ships were more than he dreamed. Two enormous warships outfitted with ice breakers and tested on previous polar expeditions, the *HMS Erebus* and the *HMS Terror* represented the peak of exploration technology at the time. Both ships had reinforced [word], [word] [word], and even steam heating for the crew’s comfort. 

5. [word] wasn’t the only field that had recently undergone significant technological advancements. In the first half of the 1800s, tinned food [word] came to the United Kingdom. Canned food offered an important advantage for long voyages: it could last for years without spoiling. Therefore, Franklin ordered 8,000 cans of tinned food for the journey from factory owner Stephen Goldner. Unfortunately, he placed the order only seven weeks before the ships were to depart. This meant that Goldner had to work frantically to complete the order and sacrificed quality for speed.

6. Despite these challenges, on May 19, 1845, the crew departed from Kent, England in high spirits, hopeful of success.

	
7. After their journey across the Atlantic Ocean, the ships encountered the whaling vessels *Prince of Wales* and *Enterprise* in Canada’s Baffin Bay. It was the last anyone would ever see of the ill-fated expedition.

###A desperate search

8. In 1847, after two years without word from Franklin or the crew, Franklin’s wife, Lady Jane Franklin, [word] the Admiralty to undertake a search for the missing ships. Although they assured her that there was no cause for alarm, they launched three search parties. 

9. Finally, in 1854, searcher John Rae made a key discovery. He met an [word] who told him of a group of White men who had perished from starvation. The Inuk even had [word] they claimed were from the missing men. Other Inuit confirmed the tales of starvation and mentioned that the men had resorted to [word]. However, when Rae returned to England and related these details, he was met with a [word] by Lady Franklin and others, who stated that the Inuit people couldn’t be trusted. In the end, the oral histories of the Inuit were largely [word].

10. In 1855, ten years after the Franklin expedition originally departed, the Admiralty decided it would fund no more search parties. Lady Franklin used her own money to sponsor one last expedition. It was this search that would reveal one of the most important clues about the fate of the *Erebus* and *Terror.*



###The Victory Point note

11. In 1859, Lieutenant William Hobson discovered what became known as the Victory Point note in a [word] on Canada’s King William Island. Written by Franklin in 1846, the note stated that the ships were trapped in ice but that all was well and the crew remained in good spirits. The note also included the coordinates where the ships had become icebound. 

12. More disturbing were the [word] written in the margins of the note. Written a year after the original note, these comments described how Franklin and 23 others had died. They added that both ships had been abandoned after being trapped in ice for 19 months. 

13. Finally, searchers had a solid clue to tell them where to start looking for the missing ships.

###A partial resolution

14. Despite the Victory Point note, the remains of the *Erebus* and *Terror* weren’t found until 2014 and 2016, respectively—over 150 years after the original expedition.
	
15. And as for the 24 men who died aboard the trapped ships? Theories abound as to the cause of their deaths. Some blame the poorly prepared tinned food, which may have been improperly sealed with lead, causing the men to die of lead poisoning. Others believe a lack of zinc in the men’s diets may have caused their deaths. Because so many of the men’s bodies are missing, that is one mystery that may never be solved completely.

16. But what happened to the 105 men who walked away from the ships, seeking a return to civilization? Research has indicated that the Inuit tales were correct all along. Marks on the bones of the recently discovered bodies are consistent with cannibalism.

questions.question_content:
**A student concludes that the Victory Point note did not result in the immediate recovery of the lost ships.**

**Which quotation from the text best supports this conclusion?**

choices.label: A
choices.content: “In 1859, Lieutenant William Hobson discovered what became known as the Victory Point note in a cairn on Canada’s King William Island.” (paragraph 11)

choices.label: B
choices.content: “The note also included the coordinates where the ships had become icebound.” (paragraph 11)

choices.label: C
choices.content: “Finally, searchers had a solid clue to tell them where to start looking for the missing ships.” (paragraph 13)

choices.label: D
choices.content: “Despite the Victory Point note, the remains of the *Erebus* and *Terror* weren’t found until 2014 and 2016, respectively…” (paragraph 14)

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
